skillName: Position negatives on a number line
misconceptionSummary: Students think −5 is greater than −1 because 5 is greater than 1.

visuals.ruleText: Negative numbers are a mirror of the positives — reflected through zero
visuals.subText: +3 is 3 steps right of zero; −3 is 3 steps left of zero

steps.narration: Every positive number has a negative mirror on the other side of zero. Three and negative three are the same distance from zero — but in opposite directions.
Moving left always decreases the value. Negative five is further from zero than negative one — so negative five is the smaller number, even though five is bigger than one as a digit.

lines.text: The bigger the negative digit, the smaller the number

steps.narration: Think of temperature. Minus ten is much colder — and much further left on the number line — than minus two. A bigger digit after the minus sign means a smaller, colder, further-left number.

visuals.label: Further left of zero → smaller value

steps.narration: Negative five is less than negative one. On the number line, it sits further to the left — and left always means smaller.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking −5 > −1 because 5 > 1. For negatives, the larger the digit, the further LEFT and the SMALLER the value.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: With negatives, size works in reverse. A bigger digit after the minus sign puts you further left — and further left always means a smaller number.
